reviews.review_text:
Finally finished it 100% after 120 hours. One of the best games I’ve ever played. I know this “delivery simulator” type of game isn’t for everyone, but for someone like me who loves story-rich games, it genuinely felt like watching a movie while playing. Definitely gonna continue with the second game later, but I need a break first and play something else while waiting for a discount so I don’t get burned out.

Gameplay — 8.5/10
Honestly, the gameplay won’t click with everyone. The early game feels pretty slow-paced, but once you reach the late game, it becomes way more fun. And there’s actually so much more content in Death Stranding than just delivering packages.

Story — 9.3/10
Almost perfect. There are still a few plot points that could’ve been executed better in my opinion, but overall the story was incredible. Classic Kojima-style plot twists, memorable characters, and emotional moments that really pull you in. My favorite performance was definitely Mads Mikkelsen — his aura was absolutely insane.

Environment / Graphics / Atmosphere — 9.6/10
As someone who really enjoys beautiful scenery and games with chill moments, this is one of Death Stranding’s biggest strengths. The visuals are absolutely stunning, and every cutscene genuinely feels like watching a movie.

One more thing… the achievement grind is brutal 😭
If you’re not fully committed don’t even try it lmao

A Long-Awaited Review of Death Stranding or Yet Another Masterpiece by the Genius Hideo Kojima?

My first experience with Death Stranding began when I played and completed Death Stranding 2. That’s when I truly fell in love with the game, and it motivated me to go back and play the first part to see where it all began.

Death Stranding 1 is a good game, but unlike the second part, the first five chapters can feel overwhelming right from the start. The creatures, the lack of transport, and the absence of weapons early in the game — compared to Death Stranding 2 — can be really frustrating. Where the sequel gives you these tools right after the prologue, the first game only unlocks them after completing chapters 3–5 (like the assault rifle or the motorcycle).

Yes, at the beginning — and honestly throughout most of the story — the game feels both challenging and engaging. There were even moments when I wanted to skip parts or just delete the game altogether.

Nevertheless, I completed it with great and can definitely recommend this masterpiece.



Долгожданный обзор Death Stranding или очередной шедевр гения Хидео Кодзимы?

Моё первое знакомство с игрой Death Stranding началось, когда я сыграл и прошёл Death Stranding 2. Тогда я просто влюбился в эту игру и это послужило мотивацией пройти первую часть и узнать всё, с чего началось.

Death Stranding 1 - это хорошая игра, но в отличие от второй части, первые 5 глав начинают тебя душить с самого начала игры. Твари, отсутствие транспорта и оружия в начале игры, в отличии от Death Stranding 2 вызывает у тебя режим абобы и взрывающегося пукана. Там где в Death Stranding 2 данные предметы отрываются после пролога, в первой же части, это открывается после завершения 3-5 главы (например штурмовая винтовка или мотоцикл).

Да, действительно в начале, да и по факту на протяжении всего сюжета игра кажется одновременно и сложной и интересной, и даже были такие моменты, когда хотелось скипнуть и удалить игру.

Тем не менее, игру я прошёл с большим удовольствием и однозначно рекомендую этот шедевр.



Довгоочікуваний огляд Death Stranding чи черговий шедевр генія Хідео Коджіми?

Моє перше знайомство з грою Death Stranding почалося з того, що я пройшов Death Stranding 2. Тоді я просто закохався в цю гру, і це стало мотивацією пройти першу частину та дізнатися, з чого все почалося.

Death Stranding 1 — це хороша гра, але, на відміну від другої частини, перші 5 глав починають душити тебе вже з самого початку гри. Твари, відсутність транспорту й зброї на початку гри, на відміну від Death Stranding 2, викликають у тебе стан повного роздратування. Там, де в Death Stranding 2 ці предмети відкриваються після прологу, у першій частині це стає доступним лише після завершення 3–5 глав (наприклад, штурмова гвинтівка чи мотоцикл).

Так, справді, на початку, та й загалом протягом усього сюжету гра здається водночас і складною, і цікавою. Навіть були моменти, коли хотілося просто пропустити або видалити гру.

Тим не менш, я пройшов її з великим задоволенням і однозначно рекомендую цей шедевр.

I played this game on PS on Release for about 2-3 hours.
I can not remember why I stopped, maybe another game came by... I dunno.

Then the Director`s Cut came out and Death Stranding 2 and I finally thought, it's time to go back.
And so far, I do not regret it at all.

This game isn't for everyone, this game is different and that is good.

It starts really slow with a lot of cutscenes, but once you are out of the prologue
the game reveals new things around every corner.

The carrying mechanics are perfect for controller.
You can feel the weight and you actually have to work to balance it.

Once you unlock vehicles the fun really starts. Build bridges, streets etc etc.
And all this comes with a passive online mode.
You can see the build of other players from your server, you can use them, you can like them, you can upgrade them.
And no, these builds are not there right from the beginning, you can only see them once you  yourself unlock these build and once you fully unlock the area.

You have to be familiar with Kojimas work, his brain works different, weird things everywhere.

And last but not least, this game features Top Notch actors.
Norman Reedus, Mads Mikkelsen, Troy Baker, Guillermo del Toro and many many famous Cameos.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yb3HHLjmzDM
If you really get into this game and understand it, there is nothing as beautiful and engaging as this. it truly is a remarkable experience.

I should have bought this game so much sooner. 
As a long time Kojima fan I don't know what put me off of buying DS or put it in my blind spot, but I truly feel like I cheated myself out of an incredible game for literal years - but at least I'm here in time for the DS revival thanks to DS2's pc port. 
Death Stranding is a beautiful game with a beautiful story. It will probably make you cry by the end. It is so much more than a "walking simulator" and I feel like it's the kind of game I can and will sink many more hours into even though i am fully 'done' with it. I wish I could replay it for the first time again, even though I literally only played it for the first time in March. 
It's not for everyone, but it doesn't have to be. I'll say this about both games, but it's true. It's not exactly like a game you've played before. You pick up cargo, you bring it from point a to point b, and you get graded on how well you take care of it. Sometimes you're timed. Sometimes it's fragile and more likely to break. And sometimes it's a bomb. Maybe even multiple sometimes.

I hate the vehicle controls and trying to navigate over terrain is horrendous. Using the truck in Mountain Knot without roads made me see red. But aside from that I'd say the game doesn't have many glaring issues. Maybe I have a bias and a habit of reloading saves when things don't go my way, though.

Do your pizza quests when they show up. You are The Great Deliverer, after all.

Was interesting at first. Graphics and mechanics are great. The tasks get repetitive and the storyline stalls about two thirds in. This will be one of a handful in my collection that don't get finished.
This is a legendary game, brilliant world to get sunk into, lots of depth to enjoy.

This game is definitely not for everyone. It's a huge slow burn, expect to spend 10-15 hours before the story really starts to move. However, once it starts moving, wow. It's amazing. The gameplay is great too, and the soundtrack is beautiful and moves me to tears.  Keep in mind that the game is very CPU intensive. All and all, you've done it again, Kojima.
Definitely a game you need to be in the right mood for. But when that mood hits.... this game hits. Lotta fun. Runs well enough on steam deck too which I appreciate.

I played this game years ago, but never made it far. The initial hours being forced into essentially watching a film (a prententious and heavy handed one at that) made me bounce off this game quick. Fast forward to now, and I finally gave it a solid chance. 

And I am glad I did.

While I feel my initial criticisms are definitely valid to this day, when this game opens up it becomes an absolute delight. Planning deliveries and routes and exploring to your hearts content in this desolate world is just an absolute treat for the eyes and ears and it is quite unlike any game I have ever experienced. 

Once you become used to an environment and start building structures it becomes your own unique world, and with the structures of others showing up as well, you feel connectied in a desolate world more and more as the game goes on. 

I would definitely give this game the chance to grow on you, but understand if you bounce off in the initial hours as well.

A very niche game and a literal walking simulator almost 90% of the time with some different ways to get around.  The story is great, I beat this on Playstation on release, but I'm replaying the Directors Cut so I can play the second.  If you're a Kojima fan, it's worth it for the story, if you're a MGS fan, it's not even close to the same.  Some interesting boss battles and intense areas of sneaking and just trying to survive.

AMAZING!!!!!!! 

Basically you deliver boxes to people. That’s it. But the overall gaming loop is beyond addictive and when it clicks, it clicks hard! 

Also helps that the games narrative is sublime. The story just drops you in the middle of things and it takes a while to understand and get going but when it does it was brilliant. 

It’s weird. It’s different. It’s Kojima at his absolute best and pushing things

Very incredible game that takes a good moment to become enjoyable. I think that attention spans are dying and because of that the game can feel sluggish at the beginning. Once the game loop clicks, it clicks. It becomes quite enjoyable and then the story really amps it to the next level. I chose to plat the game, and I still want to load up the deliveries. Maybe i'm just a mailman at heart?

This game is amazing. There is something satisfying in walking around taking in the scenes of this beautiful world while delivering goods to people. The story promoting togetherness along with relying on one another is a perfect theme to have in the world. The story with all its turns had me coming back for more.  The music is amazing; Low Roar does an amazing at making the vibes of the game refreshing. The timing of the songs are so impactful, I made sure to listen to every song that played in its entirety. Up until the end I was hooked and I will be playing the next game now that it has been ported to steam.
This game made me forget that I'm an adult with responsibilities. Obviously not everybody's cup of tea but once you're into it, there's no going back. Better plan your agenda ahead
It's a weird fun game which once u get interested on the story you get drawn into the game more I'd recommend it to everyone.

Geniunely a masterpiece holy ♥♥♥♥ Kojima
👍100000000000000 
I feel bad for people who couldn't enjoy this game because of what their fav youtuber said about it

The game is actually really good, but all of that is completely ruined by one detail that’s just infuriating from the very first second

It’s a terrible interface or rather, its sluggishness and repetition. No matter what you do, it’ll be followed by mindless comments, followed by explanations three times over and a bunch of loading screens that just keep repeating. So, you’ve come to process some material you found along the way now watch the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ cutscene with gratitude that you have seen 100 times already. 

What? What? 
You opened the meteor station? We’ll tell you five times in a row that now you can check the weather on the map (yeah, I counted, and yeah, you still won’t be able to see the weather on the map) 
The interface is beautiful and pleasant, but it’s overloaded with a bunch of dialogs that are completely unnecessary and constantly interrupt your gameplay. I came to play, but in the end I have to stare at a static interface that’s showing off its animations, and I can’t skip it. And there’s a ton of stuff in the game—it’s like I’m not playing a game but using a life service for ADHD. 

I really like the game but this ♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥♥♥ gameplay is consuming 60% of my time 

Kodzima moment and it will never change
♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, cutscene after cutscene after cutscene after cutscene, I want to play the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game not watch hideo stroke himself through these lame cinematics. Awful "game"

Beautiful game, an incredible story, and a surprisingly fun gameplay loop to tie it together. 

While it isn't jam packed with combat, the fights seemed clunky at times. The bosses themselves are a great spectacle though. 

10 / 10 Absolute masterpiece Mr Kojima, though I wish there were more feet.

Not remotely fun.

I'll happily ride a bike around doing deliveries and building things.
I will happily fight ghosts with grenades.

Why am I constantly being stopped from doing one by the other?

Why is my bike stopping for an identical cutscene to tell me there are ghosts in the area the hundredth time the game has made me go through the area?

Why does the initial attack send you miles from your bike that you just wasted your finite human lifespan organising?

It's clear from the 'balancing' mechanics that the game is supposed to be somewhat annoying, but they take it way too far for me to be willing to sit through it.

This game is happy to waste your time then act like it was trying to make a point that just never lands.

Death Stranding is a unique experience that I genuinely think everyone should play, the writing and story are 100/10, the characters are likeable and understandable, the visuals and composition are great and the soundtrack is perfect.

This game implements multiplayer in the most unique way possible, seeing the tracks/structures/equipment other players have left behind and helping each other indirectly. It's impressive that this game originally came just before COVID because it feels too spot on.

Gameplay in itself can be relaxing at times but it is engaging, people like to call this game a walking simulator but just walking is the first obstacle of many, you unlock more and more tools to traverse the varying terrain, you can tackle the same geography in different ways depending on your approach and what you are carrying. There is also different hostile NPCs that might or might not be in some areas, adding more obstacles. The deliveries you make can vary greatly, your approach to a route will be different if you need to deliver a few fragile containers, something you can't submerge on water, or a delivery of 24 heavy metal containers.

There is a few things that can be criticized though:
Despite the amazing soundtrack, they only play in specific moments of the game's story, when you are in the middle of a delivery, I don't know what Kojima was thinking but the music stops when you make Sam sit down and rest, it also stops and doesn't come back when you enter a hostile area, the problem is that a lot of the time music starts when you are about to reach your destination or when you are about to enter a hostile area, which stops the music too.
I wouldn't have a problem with this if it wasn't for the fact that the music player is in the room, but Sam doesn't carry around any music player... Why? why can't you listen to music as you like while actually playing the game? he actually did this in TPP, why not here? Only maxed out structures play music but those are set by the creator and the range is very low, and either way you are not going to spend too much time near a single place.

Then there's the human enemies, MULE's are supposed to be people that don't want to kill you, just steal your cargo, they even explained them in detail at the start of the game. But once you unlock the Terrorists that actually want to kill you and have guns... ALL MULE's NON LETHAL ARMAMENTS ARE REPLACED WITH REAL GUNS, so instead of having more variety of enemies you just get a new one that also replaces the previous ones.

Despite these and other mechanics that feel underutilized I still think you should play this game, it is a unique experience and it is very fun to play.

Good game. Visually, the game is incredible. Hiking around the beautiful landscapes is enjoyable. My only real gripe with the game is the driving. It's dreadful, especially on rocky terrain, but it's bearable with most routes you'll take. I'm not good at critiquing stories, but I enjoyed it.

Okay this game is ADVANCED. It takes a while to get used to. The beginning of the game is confusing,  complex, and I was beyond frustrated trying to understand what the hell was happening. Endless tutorial prompts and feeling completely lost in regards to the story. I would have dropped the game early if not for an encouraging voice telling me to keep going. After overcoming the learning curve and beginning to love the gameplay around when I got to Port Knot City, the narrative was the only thing that holding me back from really enjoying my time with it. I did like Deadman and BB a lot, and they helped keep me engaged with everything before a natural intrigue took over around meeting Mama, and stuck with me for the rest of the game. Thus, I was heavily rewarded with an amazing and indepth story that made itself comfortable in the back of my brain for months following completion. 

The world building is fascinating and so wonderfully deep I couldn't help but share the curiosity that drives each character in the narrative. That's the thing too, the mystery of the Death Stranding is something that is still being unravelled. Research is still ongoing in-universe and what you learn is what is the most up to date info the characters have. It makes you really feel involved in each breakthrough in research. Don't expect to be given [i] all [/i] the answers by the characters, nobody knows what's going on properly either! 

The gameplay though... it's so different from everything else out there. While that's a good thing in almost every respect, it still drives a lot of people away. However it is very rewarding and forces the player to pace themselves and appreciate their time in-game more. After getting hang of balancing my cargo, and the combat system which came to me slower than I'd like to admit, I really liked it! Oddly enough I found it addicting and I had a lot of fun pushing for every achievement after the story was finished. 

Overall, yeah it's a rough start but it's very rewarding. This kind of depth is few and far between in media and it's a wonderful experience if you're able to give the game the room it needs to bloom.
So I played this game quite a bit, and I have to say it really gets boring later in the game. I really gave this game a chance but it just drags so much.

Wonderful game. Don't listen to people who call it a "walking simulator." Death Stranding is a very, VERY long game, and it takes a while to pick up, takes a while to unlock quality of life features like roads and vehicles, but when you do, the gameplay loop becomes far more rewarding and fun. MULES are fun to fight. BTs are terrifying, interesting enemies that operate on interesting physics/logic. The story and the lore is creative, if a bit silly, but compared to the current crop of AAA sloppa out there, you can't go wrong. It's very inventive. If you're a dad you'll probably like this a lot.

Death Stranding is a very dense, narratively driven game. It has long cutscenes. Very long cutscenes (if you're familiar with MGS 4 you know what I'm talking about), but they're well directed and well written. The acting obviously is top-notch considering the talent Kojima has hired. Give it and the sequel a chance. Don't listen to people on X who dismiss it, as they likely gave up in the first few hours before the game picked up.

I gave this game a fair chance. It's 1/3 mostly pointless and repetitive cut scenes and 2/3 fetch quests rolled up into a walking/frustration simulator. The plot doesn't really get going for the first 10-20 hours (it's not a bad plot, but slow AF).  

I'd be pretty annoyed if I hadn't gotten this at a significant discount during a Steam Sale

Kojima madness.
Brilliant.
unlike anything else.
If you are a true gamer you owe it to yourself to try this,
don't comment until you have made it to chapter 4

This game is not for everyone. It is very unique in its gameplay and the emotions it tries to convey. Good infrastructure is key to maximizing efficiency. The asynchronous multiplayer brings out the feeling that we're all in this together. A little help from porters you'll never meet goes a long way in navigating the rough terrain and hostile environments. Will definitely recommend if you have the patience for it. Thanks Kojima.

Weird post apocalyptic Amazon delivery dude rescues future of America by making customers happy with a bunch of weird ♥♥♥♥ mixed in.  Really odd, interesting new game mechanics,.  Good acting and music and makes you want to play more. Odd but excellent!
Phenomenal and an absolute masterpiece of a game. Though I’m not sure why there are some stupid people talking every now and then to interrupt the gameplay.

The gamplay was fun and I really enjoyed the game up until the ending. The 2 hour cutscene ending was slow and miserable and ruined the whole game for me. I was so ready to buy the second game, but this ending completely turned me off from it

Maybe great for others, not really for me. 

Story is difficult to follow, at best. I'm not trying to watch 5 hours of lore videos to enjoy a game.
Tons of rambling, repetitive dialogue.

Environments however are beautiful! A+ in aesthetics. 
Game is very well optimized.

Allow the pace of the game to grab you. Get immersed in the writing. Read the interviews, the emails, the lore. It's a masterpiece through and through. 

The gameplay may not be for everyone but my god is the setting and story top tier.

more people need to be as brave as the people (note: not *person*. you cant trick me into giving you all the credit for the game your company made just by naming it after yourself you self-obsessed ♥♥♥♥♥♥) who made death stranding. imagine making a game with the power this game had over all of us, just by having a weird genre. "famous game man makes a game about walking??? is he stupid?????" no, just ♥♥♥♥♥♥ think about what games could potentially be and how the games of today have failed to capture even 1% of that potential and you'll start to get the picture. death stranding shows what games could be, if mass appeal wasnt the assumed goal of every game that gets made. love it or hate it, the world is undeniably a little bit more interesting for the fact that there's a AAA post-apocalyptic delivery driver game in it.

also, it's just a great game. you should play it.

Enjoyability - 10/10
Soundtrack - 10/10
Mod Content - 3/10
Difficulty - 5/10
Story - 10/10

My comfort game and one of my favorite game universes. It is so much more than people give it credit for.

Positives [+] and Negatives [-]
 + Unique delivery system-based game
+ Enjoyable combat
+ Profound story
+ Beautiful hyperrealistic open world
- [Specific to Steam Deck] Mediocre game performance
- Empty open world
- Combat can be clunky with all movement mechanics

I have watched several YouTube playthroughs of this when it first released. But never since then and I remember zero details. I just know Norman Reedus, Mads Mikkelsen, and Margaret Qualley are in the game (and apparently the Guillermo Del Toro).

The open world is visually pleasing. It does fall off on a gameplay standpoint. It’s just empty land filled with cargo waiting to be picked up. From a lore standpoint, it does make sense as the story is all about extinction and world-ending phenomenons.

The Director’s Cut is proven to run worse on the Steam Deck than the original version of the game. After minimal research, there’s old threads of Death Stranding running pretty amazing on the Steam Deck. Until it was delisted and the Director’s Cut version replaced the original and the lowered performance was already noticeable by a lot of people.

Midway through the game, I was becoming unmotivated to finish the game due to the performance on the Steam Deck. I was about to quit the game until I discovered GeForce NOW. I switched to GeForce NOW and this saved my Steam Deck playthrough of this game. I'm not here to advertise GeForce NOW, but even the free version still performs better than playing natively. When in doubt, GeForce NOW.

The way this game is designed, it isn't for everyone, for a lot of people. But I enjoyed it for what it is, I loved it for what it is, and I can't wait to continue Sam's journey in Death Stranding 2.

I was expecting this to be good, and my expectations were exceeded. There are so many games out there chasing trends, and this title felt truly original. Looking forward to seeing what comes next in Death Stranding 2. Also zero performance issues, ran amazingly well.

Give it a shot.  It's something different.  The story is out there but different.  After playing games for many, many years, I can say this was refreshing to play.  Yes the meme walking simulator has some truth to it, alot of the gameplay revolves around being a fedex contract deliverer (not literally), but there is combat you can engage in between two different main enemy types that have to be handled differently.

Brings back the good ol days of solid MGS length cutscenes.  At the end I felt fulfilled.  Now playing DS2, and it's even more of an improvement.  You can honestly jump to DS2 if you haven't played the first, and honestly would recommend this after playing DS2 because there is just more combat and that was a big complaint with this game.    But if you're not someone that needs to shoot a gun to have fun, this game may be for you.

but this a great experience.  top 15 gaming experiences for me this game was

In 2019, I didn't really think much of this game; a quirky cinematic walking sim made by Kojima, the MGS guy. Given the contemporary reviews, and my lack of a real pc, I decided that I wasnt interested. 

Well, I tried it now, and unfortunately its a cinematic masterpiece and a one of a kind video game, which is simultaneously ridiculous, and takes itself seriously, but also it does not at all, it does not. While it does feel a bit forced at times, and poorly acted, and a bit cliche, and a lot of dialogue, and a lot of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ emails, at no point does it feel like its overwhelming, or boring; on the contrary, I cant recall any major plot point where I didn't feel at least slightly amused or entertained. 

It was said in 2019, and I'm quite certain it is being said now that Kojima has the touch for movies and movie-adjacent experiences, and I'm glad to report that death stranding is about 40% by volume pure, refined cinema. The rest? building roads baby! put that ♥♥♥♥ in my veins!! idiot at edge knot city be looking like squidward when he sees me pull out my 5th grenade launcher that i acquired through begging, keep on keeping on porter! reminds me of Ride with Norman Reedus! once you've played death stranding you will never stop wanting to beat videogamedunkey to death with your bare hands.

I would like to preface my review by saying that if you are on the fence about Death Stranding, give it a shot. If you're curious enough to consider it, you should dive right in! 

I finally finished death stranding today, on the 1st of June 2026, after savouring this masterpiece of a game for 6 weeks. Truthfully.. this is the first video game to ever make me cry. Never have I ever experienced such a thought provoking, tasteful and visually stunning video game. I would even go as far as to call it art. The worldbuilding is truly unparalleled, paired with genuine and loveable characters, shockingly gorgeous scenery and an indescribable atmosphere.

Along the journey, you learn to have patience. You start to notice and appreciate the smallest things, a mushroom left from the pee of another porter, or someone else's ladder that saves you time whilst scaling a perilous mountain. My words cannot do this game justice, it's a masterpiece that you need to experience for yourself.

Death Stranding grabbed me from the very start, and now that I've finished it, i have to say that it never let me go. Thank you Kojima. 

Keep on keeping on! 

(Also if you've gotten this far, thanks for reading )

"A unique, meditative, and hauntingly beautiful experience."

Death Stranding is not your typical action game; it is a profound journey about connection, solitude, and resilience. At first, the gameplay might seem slow, but once you immerse yourself in the world, the simple act of delivering cargo becomes incredibly satisfying and therapeutic.

Pros:

Atmosphere: The landscapes and the soundtrack are absolutely breathtaking.

Story: A complex, emotional, and thought-provoking narrative that keeps you engaged until the very end.

Community: The "Strand" system is brilliant—building roads and structures to help other players creates a sense of shared purpose that is rarely seen in games.

Cons:
The slow-paced start might not be for everyone, especially those looking for constant combat.
Verdict: If you are looking for something different, artistic, and deeply impactful, this game is a must-play. It’s a masterpiece of modern gaming. Highly recommended!

I'm still in the middle of the game, but I'm already loving it. As a big Kojima fan and someone who enjoyed most of the Metal Gear games, Death Stranding has been a great experience so far.

I really enjoy the vehicles, road building, and world-building elements. There's something very satisfying about delivering cargo, improving the map, and making travel easier for yourself and other players.
🚶📦🌧️🛣️🌎🔗

The story has me hooked, the world is beautiful, and the gameplay is much more fun than I expected. Definitely one of the most unique games I've played. Looking forward to seeing how the story ends.

Kojima is a legend.
Good story, graphs, and gameplay.

+the story is really good
+the gameplay delivery is a bit fun. specially when planning your routes, items to bring, do i fight or go amazon prime runs
+the 3rd person shooter gameplay is aight, nothing new
+always use auto sort/auto arrange your cargo

-but the driving in this game, bruh.
-i keep hitting invisible walls even after building auto pavers/roads. even a small rock can make the bike go a full stop and for some reasons the front tire of the bike glitches inside the rock and needs a certain angle to dislodged it
-the tutorials are so long
-for the first few hours, the UI feels super messy

Got to play this masterpiece when Epic Games gave it to me for free. Played more than 400 hours there, and will play 400 more here as well. [h1]Kojima has done it again![/h1]
I was a bit skeptical going into this game, but I have been thoroughly blown away! The early game can be a bit confusing & frustrating (something you may share with Sam), but trust me and stick with it.
The amount of tactical freedom, pacing, & creativity the game demands is refreshing, while violence is not at the centre of the game, it is necessary.
In these times where technology overwhelms us, Kojima delivers a narrative masterpeice, where you can't help but reflect on your own 'connections', and the importance of community around us.

This just goes to show what MGSV could have been...
For me a video game is not just about being entertained or passing time, it is about meaningfulness, which can be found in abundance here.
This game felt like a journey from start to finish, not knowing what to expect next in the story kept me engaged along with its beautiful cinematics and unique story telling.

Very few games make it onto my 'Legends' steam collection. This one easily made it. I would even go so far as to say it's a top 3 game of all time for me.

In game world is fantastic, the environment (Iceland) is also fantastic, the gameplay is a perfect balance of relaxing, exciting, and rewarding. The story can be hard to follow, but is a good one none the less... very unique for sure. One of the first games that I have kept playing past the core story, and I keep playing again and again just for the vibes.

Scored on sale for $15, making the 60 hours of core gameplay very high value.
love this game , never played this type game before it really looks after newbies easy to navigate and music in game 10/10 recommend to all

I played 5 hours of Death Stranding back in 2020 (pre-Directors Cut) which was enough to be introduced to this strange world through it's opening prologue, a handful of story and side missions and from what I recalled, a large amount of beautifully detailed cut scenes. I then walked away, literally, from the game for the next 6 years. 

This itself is the key for anyone who makes the decision to play it. Initially you either "get it" or you dont. It either resonates or falls flat. That could also equate to loving it or hating it if it was just another game. But not being hooked from the initial hours spent in this world does not mean it didn't leave me with the feeling there was something original and interesting waiting to be discovered here.

Like the journey across the landscapes taken as Sam Porter Bridges, Death Stranding is a marathon, not a sprint. A fine wine, not a six pack. To appreciate it, you eventually realise that this is not a game you rush. But more importantly, you must be in the correct mindset to start that marathon. Back in 2020, I wasn't. 6 years later, I found the patience required to start this journey all over again, with the aim to finally complete it.

And what a journey it was. Death Stranding tested my patience many times with its frustrating mechanics and world elements that didn't always feel like they were working for you. It pushed my resolve to continue through occasional mundane and trivial scenarios as I stepped into the shoes of this 'delivery man' in a post apocolyptic/scifi setting. It suffers from too many endings and a need to explain every storyline instead of letting me experience them and come to my own conclusions. But for every moment that frustrated, it rewarded that committment with a payout and reward in the form of innovation and originality in gameplay that it wants you to experience (and enjoy), and balanced equally with moments where everything clicked in this beautiful world. 

Death Stranding never asks more from you than it is prepared to reward you with. If you put in the time there is an expereince here that you wont find in any other game. You can choose to ignore the psycho-babble backstory and world science or you can embrace it and dive deep into its philosophical viewpoint and the questions its asking you to consider. You can choose to fully embrace the semi-online component and assist other Porters (gamers) by crafting items and improvements to assist them across the same journey and paths they will also have to travel. You can take it or leave it. That is Death Strandings strength and sometimes its weakness when too much of a good thing drags on and on.

Death Stranding sometimes stumbles under the weight of its own load, but once it arrives at its destination there are rewards waiting for you over the next hill, around the next bend, and over the vastness of its story. Commit to it and take the journey. There is nothing else quite like it.
My OCD loves this game.  Interesting story, nice graphics, and unique gameplay make for a nice change of pace from my usual gaming.

If you can't tell by the number of hours playing this game, I 100% completed the game, and more. I agree with many people that this game is not for everyone. 

It is a slow, meditative game which is constantly straddling the line between taking itself completely seriously, and being so unserious it feels like Norman Reedus is about to turn to the camera and wink at you. The story is full of jargon and terminology unique to a world that feels both familiar, and completely alien. The fundamental game play is literally about figuring out how best to hike from point A to point B while avoiding getting attacked by roving bandits or this game's version of the undead.

However, if you are the kind of person who likes the kind of slow meditative experience Death Stranding has to offer, you won't find a better game out there. If you are willing to suspend your disbelief and fully embrace this world's peculiarities, you will find something truly special "at the bottom of everything." Lastly, if you don't mind the idea of building up the world around you, one delivery and bit of infrastructure at a time, you'll find that not only are you getting fulfillment out of putting the pieces back together, but you're doing it with a community of thousands of other people helping along the way. 

There are a few interesting boss fights, and you can find stealth gameplay if you're looking for it. That's almost not the point though. There are plenty of better choices if your primary goal is to fight off Lovecraftian horrors with fairly standard guns and grenades. There are plenty of other games which handle stealth in a more compelling way. If high octane action is what you're expecting, I would suggest you temper those expectations a bit. The game has its moments of action, but they fit within a world that is much bigger than a theme park which drags you from one combat encounter to the next. 

Instead, this game's real focus is meditating on the moments of lonely beauty. What Kojima wants to teach the player more than anything, is that there is something really special in the kind of person who is happy to plant trees for others to experience their shade. We need people who are willing to break the desire to just focus inwardly on ourselves and shut out the problems of the world. 

With all of the doom and gloom on the horizon, what the world really needs is someone who can actually deliver a pizza to our hidden bunker in 30 minutes or less without flipping it over and letting it fold back in on itself. Can you be that person?

"This is more than just a game... it is an eXPERIENCE-"

Nawh jokes aside, the game is good for what it is. The best way I can describe it is an exploration game. Hike back and forth across the landscape, discovering and mastering the dangers you come across, all while experiencing a long story with very cinematic cutscenes. It is a game, but it also delivers a movie in between your game, and kudos for this game finding a balance between the two!

Dangers involve horror-inspired supernatural elements, but I wouldn't call this a horror game. There are action elements, especially later in the game. You start off as just a delivery dude but by the end you've got access to traps, trucks, and even guns to a certain extent. Some of the obstacles as you travel are as simple as navigating the landscape; no flat plain ground here, you got rocks n rivers n mountain n sht to climb.

Watch some gameplay vids if you're unsure about buying it. If you think you will like it, I highly recommend getting the directors cut over the OG, as there is a feature that genuinely makes combat more enjoyable/manageable in the early game.

Some may be inclined to call this a walking simulator, but I'm a sucker for a good view and this game is as beautiful as beautiful can be.

Kojima's weird brain is a bonus.

Such a unique and incredible experience. Kojima tried to do something completely new, something not tied to any bounds and different from anything else out there and with this game, he did exactly this. 

Before I tried the game, I read a lot of opinions from other people, saying stuff like "this is just a walking simulator" and "it's boring without any depth", but oh boy, it's so much more than that. Kinda hard to explain though without trying it out.

At the start, you deliver packages just by foot, nothing fancy apart from maybe a ladder or a climbing rope. The game is very slow and the terrain difficult, you have to manage your weight, pick the correct gear and route and balance your cargo, but step by step, you get to the destination. When you finally delivered the first cargo after traversing this beautiful landscape, it feels like an actual achievement. 

Later on, the game opens up. You can build structures to help you out with the logistics, use a lot of different equipment to make your life easier, use vehicles, build zip-line networks and roads, etc. The game also includes a way to "socialize" with the community. Each structure you've built could potentially spawn into the game of another player, so not only do you help yourself but also others. If you, for example, accumulated and added a lot of materials to build a road, this exact road can be used by some others aswell. The same works the other way around. For the work and everything you achieve, you receive likes both from other players aswell as NPCs which acts as reward or in-game currency and increases your rank and character. 

All of that might sound like a very chill experience, but don't get me wrong here. There are a couple of different enemy types in the game increasing the overall tension while you try to delivery your cargo. Some of them are just humans trying to steal your packages, others ... well, I won't spoiler anything, but it can get quite scary from time to time, at least at the beginning without proper equipment.

All of that paired with the incredible (but complex!) story makes this game a must-play. It's definitely not a game for everyone, but if you generally like the idea, give it a try and a couple of hours. It might take a bit until it clicks, but when it does, you're in for an incredible experience.

What a journey this has been. I've been playing this game for a long time and never beat it, just kept resetting progress putting it down for too long and coming back to rinse and repeat, but I finally did it. Death Stranding showed me how to slow down and appreciate the world around me, now I find myself playing other games like that. Not being in a dead sprint to the finish line, just strolling along and smelling the roses and that really showed me what I love most about games is the journey. The feeling of really immersing myself in the world and empathizing with the characters along the way. This feels almost like writing a eulogy for an old friend at this point, but as this is a review I must say, buy this game if you feel like taking time away to sit with yourself in a vast, desolate and lonely world full of beauty, horror and Kojima-isms. Love ya Sam. Keep on keeping on.

Wow.  That's the review: Wow.

But seriously though, this game has the single most ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up story I've ever heard, and it's really quite fascinating, at least to me.  I've seen this game criticized for being slow and/or boring, but that criticism misses the mark as far as I'm concerned.  In every conceivable way, when I sit down to play this game, I feel the same way as when I'm binge watching a show on Netflix.  That's not something I come across with any frequency; it doesn't even feel like a game.  The bizarre sci-fi elements with sprinklings of horror really work for the in-game universe too.

One thing to keep in mind:  the music in this game is divisive in the extreme.  Some people love it, some people hate it, and there's really not a ton of in-between, as far as I can tell.  At key points during gameplay, the camera will zoom out and music will begin playing as you navigate the landscape.  The music is what I would describe as that semi-European Indie music from the late 2000's through the mid 20 teens.  I think it's some of the worst music I've ever heard in my life, but you may feel differently, and that's not only OK, but I encourage it.  A lot of people think the music is a pivotal centerpiece for just the whole idea of what the game is trying to accomplish in the first place.  You do you, my dude.

If you do not like the indie music, as soon as it starts playing: Menu -> System -> Save -> Load Latest Save Data.  You'll be in exactly the same place, but the music will not play once the game reloads.  It literally takes less than 8 seconds.

To summarize: Fantastic game, S-tier story, divisive music that you may or may not like.  I would say this is a required purchase if on sale, for sure.  But I will also say that I bought Death Stranding 2 at full release price, before I even finished this game, for what that's worth.

The breakout game from Hideo Kojima as his first title after being let go from Konami.
As a huge Kojima fan, I was stoked when the trailer dropped for this game. It was one of the big reasons that made me go out and get a Playstation 4. Now, years later, I am not only able to enjoy this masterpiece I am also about to play it on PC! Top that off with the added features and changes of the director's cut version and it really makes this game not one to miss.

Death Stranding is an action-adventure game where you take on the role of Sam Bridges. A porter (think postman) after an apocalyptic event called the "Death Stranding" causes spectral, near invisible creatures called Beached Things to roam the earth. They also bring about a dreadful rain called Timefall that rapidly ages anything that it comes in contact with. Top all that off with the fact that now if someone dies, their body turns into a necrotic bomb unless disposed of within a few hours. The Death Stranding is truly an extinction level event.
Sam is tasked with relinking all the fractured settlements and survivors into one, complete a network of information. He accepts deliveries and orders to make his way westward to bring the remains of America back together and hopefully find a way to stop everything from falling apart. One step at a time.

To start, the game is a slow burn. Death Stranding is not a "weekend" game and honestly it deserves not to be. The isolation and arduous nature of the game is there to impose how the world would be during such a journey.  You mainly make your trek on foot. You sneak by enemies holding your breath, doing your best not to alert them. You plan your movements across rushing water, up steep cliffs and down rocky valleys.
Though things seem tough, as you progress through the story and unite more cities and settlements you start getting schematics for handy tools to help. It starts simple with ladders and climbing ropes but slowly you come into vehicles such as bikes and trucks, floating cargo carriers and robotic legs, and even a few nifty firearms to keep the BT's at bay.
Even though Sam is alone, he's not really "alone". As other players/porters build things in their games, some of that is shared into your game as well, and vice versa. Players making the same route as you might build a shelter from the Timefall or even have ladders and ropes already placed down. Signs to lift your spirits and stone cairns to mark where they rest, and even rebuilt roads to make travel so much more easier.
They can even leave behind cargo for you. Sometimes things are lost in transit, maybe something fell off their packs and they didn't want to stop and pick it up. Or a choice between hoarding materials or delivering a package on time. Whatever the reason, you can take a piece of lost cargo and keep it for yourself, or hell, turn it in for some likes! Yes, likes! The main way to say thank you and you're welcome in this world is by sending likes. You can like a sign, or a well placed zip-line, you can even like the area where someone last peed! Yep, show your support to your fellow porter with some likes!

Outside of it's unique gameplay, Death Stranding "delivers" in to two other major departments. The cast and the soundtrack.  Norman Reedus takes the helm as Sam Bridges and next to him you have the wonderful talents of Mads Mikkelsen, Guillermo del Toro, Lea Seydoux, Troy Baker, Margaret Qualley and even some smaller appearances from Junji Ito and Conan O'Brien in the mix. All of these actors bring so much life to their respective characters and gives the game a real personality.
The soundtrack is also on another level. Low Roar is the main band that is featured in a lot of segments in the game. I can say that I never heard of them until I played this game, but I am glad I got to experience them.
The low, sober scores that play during key moments of the journey really do bring in that feeling of isolation. making your way down a foggy, rocky valley and "I'm Leaving" starts playing brings a sense of emotion that I can't even begin to describe.

Even with it's uniqueness the game does have it's flaws. The pacing is one of them. The game has 15 chapters. While exposition and story are decently laid out for the first 5 or 6 chapters, once chapter 10 hits is just snowballs and snowball to the end. This is Kojima, though. From Metal Gear to Policenauts, Kojima always throws everything at you in the end.
The other thing is kinda-sorta the gameplay. I love the game, but it is 110% a walking simulator in the most literal sense. Outside of the final 5 chapters, action and combat are rare. Add the fact that when combat does happen, guns are clunky and the hit reticles are a guess at best.
Most of your time you are just planning routes, making sure you have the gear you need and seeing how much you can deliver in a few trips.
Lastly, controlling Sam can be frustrating at times. Death Stranding utilizes every button on the controller, which is what a game should do. Though it does it in a very convoluted way. Simply changing guns or active equipment requires about 4 or 5 inputs alone. I found myself switch from controller to mouse & keyboard for combat and going back again when I am doing everything else.

Wrapping it up, Death Stranding is an amazing game that I really do hope many people get to experience. Being Kojima's first game away from Konami I am glad he was able to find sturdy footing. I am glad I got to play this on the PS4 and I am glad I got to play the director's cut on PC.
If you're on the fence, feel free to wait for a sale, but this game deserves it even at full price!

Sometimes the game ignores already seen animations or hints which is a little annoying.

The text on the screen is too much at times, I'd rather see the game and have to use my brain instead of getting bombarded with info I don't need or already know.

The UI and driving feel funky, maybe even a little bad. I need to do so many clicks just to do something. Shortcuts could do wonders.  

The music is awesome, but when it plays it's usually not timed well. I always seemed to get to places faster and I couldn't enjoy the music for long enough for me to be attached. I wish for a music player out in the wilderness. (I think the second one has one though)

There's some stuff that seemed like forgotten strands (haha) of story. (e.g.: the levels of the DOOMS afflicted)

Game's really pretty though, I stopped many times to admire the sights.

Also, after like 60% of the game, I got kinda bored of it. I know that's one of the vibes they were looking for, but it was a bit hard for me to push and finish it. The story and visuals saved this aspect as I was curious about the lore and wanted to experience the vibe of the game too much to stop halfway.

The fights at the end are awesome. And the scenes where the characters look into your eyes and speak to you... Very original, I haven't see that one yet. I really felt the connection to the characters in those scenes.

I recommend to people who're not looking for a thrill, but want an experience.
nice cutscenes and story, boring gameplay

An unforgettable journey from start to finish. Death Stranding offers a unique and emotional story, stunning graphics, and incredibly immersive gameplay. The world feels mysterious and beautiful, and every delivery feels meaningful. The soundtrack is amazing, and the connection you build with BB makes the experience even more special. A truly one-of-a-kind masterpiece that every gamer should experience. Highly recommended! 🌟🎮

Note - this is the first time I've played a Hideo Kojima game, so some of these game features may be common.

Death Stranding is one of the most introspective games that I've ever played.  At around 30 hours into the game, I can see why there might have been criticism that it is a walking simulator.  It's not, but a better description of the actual gameplay is similar to a logistics or transport tycoon game where you are rewarded for each delivery you make. 

You start off as a simple porter walking your deliveries from place to place, using only the occasional ladder or climbing rope to get to where you need to be.  You will at first avoid, and eventually confront the monsters and men that take up entire regions of the map that you have to traverse to.  At each destination, you'll be thanked by those that you deliver to, and slowly connect an otherwise shattered world.

The asynchronous multiplayer aspect of this game is put to great use.  You'll never see other players, but the structures they add will be added here and there to your world also.  There's nothing like making a difficult trek to a new area, only to discover a helpful bridge or climbing rope that was left by a trailblazer before you.  You end up quietly thanking those other people who you'll never meet, and that is when I think the real impact of the game hits.  It might feel as if the game is trying to make you suffer through the difficulty in transit and obstacles in your way, but then at some point you start to realize that instead the game is reducing the suffering of others that come after you.  Making the world a better place   while learning to appreciate those that came before.

I don't know yet where this story will lead, but until then Sam and I will just keep on keeping on.

Pros
-The landscape and visuals are awesome.  I have no doubt that the rest where you are mechanic was created to just sit back a moment and enjoy the environment where you are. 
-The music and sound design is fantastic.  The music selections never fail to hit an emotional point when they play, and the silence that you become used to otherwise is used to great effect.
-The story is engaging, and despite the numerous mundane cutscenes for things like loading boxes or recycling trash, I feel like the characters are people that I want to understand and to get to know better.
-The impact that the player has on the world is important.  Once unlocked, you'll be building bridges, vehicles, roads, and more in order to become more efficient in your numerous deliveries.

Cons
-The tutorial section of the game is quite long.  You will have to put several hours into the game before leaving the first area.
-There are tons of short cutscenes for mundane tasks.  It takes multiple button presses to just recycle materials or turn in deliveries.  
-Characters act like they’ve known you for a long time, but have not (technically) been introduced yet.
-Oddities that may have nothing to do with the plot are left unanswered or even un-noted on, such as:
          Why does that guy wear a skull mask?  Does no one else in the room think that's odd?
          Why nothing said to Fragile after she caused Sam to lose his bike in the opening cutscene?
